data.title: The King of Leverage Fades: BitMEX Dies, Perpetual Contracts Live On
data.description:
Written by: Xiaobing

On July 23, BitMEX released a farewell letter on its official website.

The exchange will cease trading services on September 23, 2026. No further specific reasons were given, only that the board of directors, after reviewing the company and the entire crypto industry, decided to close the exchange.

In today's crypto market, BitMEX can no longer be considered a mainstream exchange. Binance, OKX, and Bybit dominate the centralized contract market, while on-chain platforms like Hyperliquid have attracted a new generation of traders. Many newcomers to the industry are even unaware of BitMEX.

But its exit still deserves careful recording. Today, almost all the most important products of crypto exchanges, perpetual contracts, were productized and introduced to the entire industry by BitMEX. It also brought high leverage, funding rates, mark prices, and automatic liquidation to the crypto market, shaping the way trading has been done for the next decade. If stablecoins brought the US dollar into the crypto world, then perpetual contracts represent a different, opposite path: a financial product that matured in the crypto market was adopted by traditional finance. Exchanges will die, but perpetual contracts will not. This is perhaps the most dignified obituary BitMEX left for the industry. Three people, one hundred times leverage. In 2014, Arthur Hayes, a former trader at Deutsche Bank and Citibank, registered a company called BitMEX in Hong Kong, short for Bitcoin Mercantile Exchange. The partners were mathematician Ben Delo and programmer Samuel Reed. Three people, one vision: To bring Wall Street's derivatives trading to Bitcoin and to offer leverage levels Wall Street wouldn't dare dream of. One hundred times leverage. In the world of traditional finance, retail investors typically have leverage of 2 to 5 times, while futures professionals are limited to 20 times. BitMEX offered 100x leverage, meaning a 1% price reversal would wipe out the entire position. Critics called it a "Bitcoin casino," but Hayes never defended himself, publicly wearing T-shirts emblazoned with "100x," using the casino's neon lights as brand assets. The early crypto market provided fertile ground for this radicalism. There was no regulation, no KYC; an email address was all it took to open an account, and gamblers and traders from around the world flocked to the same order book. By 2019, BitMEX's daily trading volume exceeded $16 billion, and it moved into the Cheung Kong Center in Hong Kong, renting what was then the most expensive office in Asia, right below Li Ka-shing's company. That July, Hayes debated with "Dr. Doom" Nouriel Roubini in Taipei, to a packed house. A Wall Street outcast, relying on an offshore casino, had risen to a position where he could spar with mainstream economists. This was the pinnacle of BitMEX, and also the pinnacle of the old era of the crypto industry: Brutality, exorbitant profits, and only a time zone away from the iron fist of regulation. A single contract rewrote the market structure. Viewing BitMEX merely as a casino will cause you to miss some crucial information. In May 2016, BitMEX launched XBTUSD, the first perpetual contract in human financial history. To understand its significance, one must first understand the problems of traditional futures: futures have expiration dates, settle quarterly, and traders constantly roll over positions, fragmenting liquidity across contracts for different months, like a river divided into sections by a dam. Perpetual contracts remove all these dams. They have no expiration date, can be held indefinitely, and are anchored to the spot price through a mechanism called funding rates: if the contract price is higher than the spot price, the long position pays a small fee to the short position every eight hours; if it's lower, the opposite happens. The greater the price deviation, the higher the rate, attracting arbitrageurs who enter the market and pull the price back to the anchor point. No delivery, no rollover—a single river flows from beginning to end, with all liquidity converging into the same pool. The brilliance of this design lies in replacing the entire complex delivery and settlement system of traditional futures with a simple economic incentive. Its far-reaching impact can only be clearly seen within a larger context: stablecoins solve the "cash" problem in the crypto world, allowing the US dollar to circulate on-chain in token form; perpetual contracts solve the "risk transfer" problem, allowing anyone to express their opinion on prices at any time and in any direction. In the past decade or so of the crypto industry, truly original financial engineering projects that have been reverse-engineered and transferred to traditional finance can be counted on one hand, and these two are at the forefront. The adoption curve is the best evidence. Perpetual contracts first devoured the crypto derivatives market: Binance, OKX, and Bybit copied them entirely; FTX rose to prominence because of them; Hyperliquid brought them to the blockchain; now, the trading volume of crypto derivatives far surpasses that of spot trading, with perpetual contracts being the dominant force. Then, traditional finance also began to revisit this "invention of offshore casinos": US regulators publicly discussed introducing perpetual contracts into regulated markets, and compliant exchanges lined up to apply for listing perpetual products. In May 2026, the US Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) officially approved the listing of Bitcoin perpetual contracts, with Kalshi and Coinbase being the first to receive approval. Students have become teachers; the tools invented by casinos are being repackaged by people in suits and reintegrated into mainstream finance. In the script of rise and fall, BitMEX's turning point has two precise dates. On March 12, 2020, global markets crashed, and Bitcoin plummeted from nearly $8,000 to $3,600. Long positions on BitMEX experienced a series of liquidations, the liquidation engine poured sell orders into the thin order book, selling pressure overwhelmed buy orders, and the price fell out of control. Amidst the market's deepest fears, BitMEX announced a "hardware failure" and shut down for maintenance. During the downtime, prices on other exchanges stopped falling and rebounded. FTX founder SBF later said that if BitMEX hadn't shut down, the price of Bitcoin might have gone to zero. This incident made it clear to the entire market that this exchange's liquidation mechanism had become so powerful that it could single-handedly determine the fate of Bitcoin. Also starting from March 12th, the window of opportunity for competitors opened, with Binance, Bybit, and FTX continuously eroding its market share over the following year. On October 1, 2020, an even heavier hammer fell. The U.S. Department of Justice and the CFTC simultaneously indicted Hayes, Delo, Reed, and executive Dwyer for violating the Bank Secrecy Act. The core charge was knowingly refusing to establish anti-money laundering and KYC systems when U.S. users were transacting on their platforms. Reed was arrested in the U.S., while Hayes fled to Singapore before returning to the U.S. to surrender. The three founders collectively resigned from management and pleaded guilty. Hayes received a suspended sentence and house arrest, while Delo, once Britain's youngest self-made billionaire, also pleaded guilty and received a sentence. At the company level, BitMEX pleaded guilty and was fined an additional $100 million by FinCEN in early 2025. In March 2025, Trump pardoned four people, legally closing the matter, but the commercial death penalty had already been carried out five years earlier. After implementing mandatory KYC, BitMEX lost its most original moat: anonymity and no barriers to entry. It couldn't compete with Binance's scale, Bybit's product iterations, or the on-chain native Hyperliquid. Its market share shrank from absolute dominance at its peak to a small, barely visible block in charts. A Long Farewell In its final six years, BitMEX had four CEOs. Hayes was followed by Höptner, who left during the 2022 bear market, and Lutz took over. In early 2025, the company was reported to be seeking a sale, but a year and a half later, no buyer was willing to take it over. At the end of June 2026, Lutz, CFO Steiner, and growth officer Polansky all resigned on the same day without any official announcement; the truth was pieced together from changes in their LinkedIn profiles. Wilkinson, who took over as CEO, had a legal background, and the market immediately understood the signal: a ship captained by a lawyer usually ends up at a dockside scrap yard, and this time, even the scrap yard couldn't find a buyer. Looking back, BitMEX's life is a complete specimen of the crypto industry's wild era: a window of regulatory arbitrage, a group of smart people who understood derivatives, an original product that rewrote the market structure, a belated but inevitable enforcement action, a loss of market share that no one could escape, and a final curtain call with no takers. After September 23, bitmex.com will become an empty domain. Meanwhile, on trading terminals around the world, funding rates will still be settled every eight hours, with longs paying shorts, or shorts paying longs, as punctual as the tides. The company that invented this tidal system has gone bankrupt, but the tidal system itself continues to propel the entire market forward.

data.title: After oil prices hit $96, tech stocks and cryptocurrencies should be wary.
data.description:
Oil Prices, Interest Rate Hikes, and Tech Earnings: The Market is Trading in a "Return to Inflation" Today's market appears somewhat fragmented. On one hand, Asian tech stocks continue to rise, with the South Korean stock market surging, led by Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix, as the market continues to trade on the logic of US tech giants' ever-expanding AI capital expenditures. On the other hand, crude oil has climbed back above $96 per barrel, US Treasury yields have risen significantly, and the market has even begun discussing Fed rate hikes again. These two factors together constitute the core contradiction in the current market: the AI ​​industry cycle is still on an upward trajectory, but macro liquidity is deteriorating again. Over the past two years, the market's favorite narrative has been "declining inflation—Fed rate cuts—easing liquidity—expansion in tech stock and crypto asset valuations," but now this chain may be reversing.

I. Oil Prices Regain their Core Role in Global Asset Pricing

Today, the most noteworthy event isn't how much a particular company's stock price has risen, but rather that Brent crude has climbed above $96 per barrel, reaching a near six-week high. A follower asked me on Weibo, "Memories of the Chess Game," about oil prices on OKX and Gate. In the crypto market, prices are generally considered "dark oil," with variations depending on the delivery month. I usually look at the September figures, but JinTou.com updated its crude oil prices today, and the mainstream market data currently shows the October figures.

The reasons driving up oil prices are not complicated: the escalating situation in the Middle East has put both the Strait of Hormuz and the Bab el-Mandeb Strait, two important energy transport routes, at risk. Tanker detours and increased transportation costs have led the market to re-price the possibility of supply disruptions.

It's important to note that the impact of rising oil prices on the market is not simply a "positive for oil stocks."

Rising crude oil prices will first push up the costs of gasoline, transportation, electricity, and chemicals, and then gradually transmit to commodity and service prices. If oil prices remain high for a sufficient period, the inflation previously suppressed by the Federal Reserve may rebound. The most favorable sector for stocks is the coal chemical industry chain. Furthermore, with rising inflation, US short-term Treasury yields rose significantly today, with the 10-year Treasury yield rising to approximately 4.65%, and the market is even starting to bet again on a Fed rate hike in September. For risk assets, the most troublesome issue is never the "25 basis point rate hike" itself, but rather the market's overall reassessment of the interest rate path. Previously, the market was thinking: How many more rate cuts can there be this year? Now, the market is starting to think: Will the Fed raise rates again? These two issues have completely different impacts on valuations. The second key point of the Fed's meeting next week is not whether to raise interest rates. The Fed's next policy meeting will be held on July 28-29. The current target range for the federal funds rate is 3.50% to 3.75%, and the June meeting chose to keep rates unchanged. Based on the current situation, the probability of the Fed raising interest rates immediately at the July meeting remains low. The truly important questions are: 1. How will the Federal Reserve assess the inflationary risks posed by rising oil prices? 2. Will it acknowledge that the room for interest rate cuts this year has further narrowed? 3. Will it retain the policy option of raising interest rates again in September? Many cryptocurrency investors only look at the final interest rate decision and easily overlook the wording in the press conference. In fact, for highly volatile assets like Bitcoin, "hawkish expectations" often have an impact earlier than the actual interest rate hike. Recent research has also found that Bitcoin is very sensitive to hawkish statements from central banks, and changes in policy expectations are often reflected in prices before actual interest rate adjustments. Therefore, what the Federal Reserve says in the coming week may be more important than what it does. If the Federal Reserve only says it will "continue to observe," risk assets still have some buffer. If the Federal Reserve explicitly emphasizes energy prices, a second wave of inflation, and further policy tightening if necessary, then US Treasury yields and the dollar may continue to strengthen, putting pressure on tech stocks and the crypto market. III. Tech Earnings Are Not the Problem, the Problem Is Excessive Spending. Yesterday, the market focused on the earnings reports of Google and Tesla. Google's cloud business continues to show strong growth, but the market's focus has shifted from "how much revenue has grown" to "how much more money will be spent on AI." This AI rally has entered its second phase. In the first phase, as long as a company announced the purchase of GPUs, the construction of data centers, and increased investment in AI, the stock price would rise. In the second phase, the market is starting to ask: When will all this capital expenditure translate into profits? The sharp drop in stock price after Google announced its Q2 2026 results is a clear indication of this. This is actually a short-term boon for the Asian semiconductor supply chain. This is because regardless of whether Google, Microsoft, and Meta ultimately make enough money from AI, orders for storage, servers, advanced packaging, power equipment, and data center suppliers have already been secured. In other words, tech giants bear the risk of returns, while those selling the shovels get the revenue first. Today, South Korea's KOSPI surged by over 3%, led by Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix, precisely reflecting this trading logic. However, investors must understand that the "selling the shovels" logic is not a perpetual motion machine. Once tech giants believe that the return on AI investment is lower than expected and cut capital expenditures, the profit expectations of the semiconductor and memory supply chains will quickly reverse. Therefore, when observing financial reports, we should not only look at revenue and earnings per share, but also focus on four key indicators: AI capital expenditure guidance, cloud business growth rate, data center utilization rate, and management's description of the investment return cycle.

IV. For the cryptocurrency market, the most important thing to be wary of is dollar liquidity

Bitcoin has recently been fluctuating around $65,000 to $66,000. The market is paying attention to the progress of the US cryptocurrency regulatory bill on the one hand, and digesting the situation in the Middle East and changes in interest rate expectations on the other.

Currently, there are two completely opposite logics in the cryptocurrency market.

The optimistic logic is that rising geopolitical risks may attract some "digital gold" buying to Bitcoin, and the gradual clarification of US cryptocurrency regulations is also conducive to the entry of long-term institutional funds.

The pessimistic logic is that rising oil prices drive up inflation, the Federal Reserve turns hawkish again, the dollar and US Treasury yields rise, global liquidity tightens, and high-volatility assets are sold off. Historically, the second logic is usually more direct in the short term. Gold can be traded relatively purely as a safe haven, but Bitcoin still possesses the attributes of a safe-haven asset, a technology asset, and a highly leveraged liquid asset. When the dollar index, US Treasury yields, and market volatility rise simultaneously, Bitcoin is often priced as a risk asset first, not as digital gold. Therefore, what's most worth watching in the crypto market right now is not just whether Bitcoin breaks through a certain technical level, but rather: whether oil prices can continue to rise, whether US Treasury yields continue to reach new highs, and whether market expectations for a September rate hike further intensify. If oil prices fall rapidly and inflation trading cools, Bitcoin and tech stocks could potentially recover. If Brent crude continues to approach $100, the Fed's policy pressure will increase, limiting the upside potential of risk assets. V. What the Market Should Focus On Next Week 1. The Federal Reserve's July 28-29 Interest Rate Meeting. This is likely the most important event of the coming week. Don't just focus on whether there's a rate hike; pay close attention to whether the Fed abandons its rate cut expectations and whether it's paving the way for a September rate hike. 2. The European Central Bank's Policy Stance. Rising energy prices typically have a more direct impact on Europe than on the US. The current market expects the European Central Bank to maintain interest rates for the time being, but may retain the possibility of a rate hike in September. 3. Tech Giant Earnings and Capital Expenditure: The market is no longer satisfied with "AI revenue growth" and is beginning to demand that companies prove their AI investments can generate sufficiently high returns. Any combination of increased capital expenditures but decreased profit margins in subsequent tech earnings reports could lead to significant stock price volatility. 4. Intel and Semiconductor Supply Chain Earnings: As AI capital expenditures continue to rise, the market will further verify the strength of demand for servers, storage, and data centers. The key to earnings trading is not absolute performance, but how much better the performance is relative to high market expectations. 5. Middle East Situation and Oil Prices: If shipping through the Strait of Hormuz or the Bab el-Mandeb Strait is further disrupted, oil prices breaking through $100 will not be difficult. The real danger is not a single-day surge, but rather oil prices remaining high for several weeks, thus re-entering inflation data. VI. Current Market Trading Strategy This is neither a one-sided bullish nor a simple bearish view. The AI ​​industry trend is not over yet. Capital expenditures by tech giants are being transmitted to the semiconductor, storage, power, and data center supply chains. These companies still have real orders and profits to support them. However, the macroeconomic environment is not as favorable as it was a few months ago, and there is a clear tendency for a bubble to burst. When oil prices rise, US Treasury yields increase, and expectations of interest rate hikes intensify simultaneously, the margin for error for overvalued assets will decrease rapidly. The market may show significant differentiation going forward: AI infrastructure companies with orders, cash flow, and realized profits may remain strong. Tech stocks and altcoins that rely solely on storytelling and valuation expansion may be the first to face pressure. Energy, oil services, shipping, coal chemicals, and some resource commodities will benefit from rising prices. Aviation, downstream chemicals, logistics, and energy-intensive industries will face cost pressures. The same applies to the cryptocurrency market. Bitcoin may be more resilient than most altcoins, and there are still structural opportunities in regulatory themes such as stablecoins and the tokenization of real-world assets. However, the risks of high-leverage and purely narrative-driven cryptocurrencies are rising. Finally, a few words: The most worrying thing in this market round is not a company's earnings report falling short of expectations, nor is it a short-term drop in Bitcoin prices. What truly needs attention is whether the underlying narrative of the global market is shifting from a "rate cut trade" to an "inflation and rate hike trade." Over the past two years, people have become accustomed to increasingly loose liquidity. Now, with oil prices back above $96, US Treasury yields rising, and the Fed's rate hikes back into discussion, it's clear that the macroeconomic environment is changing. For traders, the most dangerous thing isn't misjudging a single candlestick, but rather using the logic of the previous phase to trade the next phase of the market. In the coming week, pay less attention to emotional slogans and more attention to four key data points: Crude oil, US Treasury yields, the US dollar index, and Fed rate hike expectations. If these four indicators don't weaken, every rise in tech stocks and cryptocurrencies is closer to a game of maneuvering under liquidity pressure than confirmation of a new full-blown bull market. As I said yesterday, crude oil will drain everything, and crude oil and related stocks will be the only main theme in the market.

data.title: The inventor of perpetual contracts takes his final bow: The rise and fall of BitMEX and its ultimate fate.
data.description:
Written by: Shannon@Jinse Finance

On July 23, 2026, BitMEX announced that it will officially close its exchange services on September 23, 2026 at 04:00 UTC, and will immediately cease all new user registrations. The board of directors of BitMEX's parent company, HDR Global Trading Limited, made the decision to close the exchange after a strategic review of the business and the broader crypto industry.

Today, it is with a heavy heart that we announce that the BitMEX exchange will officially cease operations on September 23, 2026 at 04:00 UTC. HDR Global Trading Limited, the owner and operator of BitMEX, made this difficult decision after a strategic assessment of the business.

While BitMEX may be different today than it once was, we remain proud of our 11-year history and the role we have played in the development of the cryptocurrency industry. We pioneered 100x leveraged perpetual contracts, which was undoubtedly the first step for most users to embark on their cryptocurrency trading journey.

Today, it has become the most traded financial product in the cryptocurrency industry, widely adopted by tens of thousands of users and exchanges. We are always proud of our robust security infrastructure, which has enabled us to maintain a perfect record of zero losses from hacking attacks throughout our operations. We assure you that your assets will remain safe and under your complete control during this transition period. This announcement is intended to allow all users ample time to ensure a smooth withdrawal process. Starting today, we strongly recommend that all users close their positions and withdraw their funds as soon as possible. BitMEX has been a cradle for many of today's top traders. Our team has poured tremendous effort and passion into building the platform into what it is today, and we are delighted to have connected with some of you over the past 11 years. Thank you to every user who has traded with us, supported us, and grown with us—thank you for your trust. The BitMEX Team. Eleven years. From inventing perpetual contracts to announcing its demise. This platform, which once dominated the global crypto derivatives market, has come to an end with a single announcement.

I. The Rise of BitMEX: A Wall Street Man Changing the Rules of Crypto Trading in Hong Kong

The story begins with a Detroit man who had worked as a derivatives trader at Deutsche Bank and Citibank for many years.

Arthur Hayes left Citibank in 2013 to start trading Bitcoin, seeing an opportunity to build a professional-grade derivatives platform.

In 2014, Hayes, along with Benjamin Delo, Samuel Reed, and Gregory Dwyer, co-founded BitMEX (Bitcoin Mercantile Exchange) in the Seychelles, operating through HDR Global Trading Limited, registered in Seychelles.

BitMEX was not an ordinary spot exchange from its inception. Hayes directly applied his derivatives thinking, accumulated in traditional finance, to a virtually unregulated emerging market.

The Birth of Perpetual Contracts BitMEX's most profound contribution to crypto history is the invention of the perpetual swap. "BitMEX invented the 100x leveraged perpetual contract, the most traded product in the crypto industry—now used by thousands of people and exchanges." A perpetual contract is a futures contract with no expiration date, pegging its price to the spot price through a funding rate mechanism. This design is ingenious: it retains the leverage of futures while eliminating the operational friction of traditional futures contracts requiring rollover or settlement upon expiration. Before this, derivatives were virtually nonexistent in the crypto market; Hayes, with the perspective of a financial engineer, created an entire new asset class. The Era of Domination At the height of market expansion in 2019, BitMEX's annual trading volume exceeded $1 trillion, accounting for approximately 57% of the global crypto derivatives market. In July 2018, BitMEX's daily trading volume reached a record high of $8 billion, with over 1 million Bitcoins traded daily, breaking industry records. BitMEX at that time was the Wall Street trading floor of the crypto world—operating 24/7, offering 100x leverage, and virtually no identity verification requirements. Global traders flocked to it, and the dramatic price fluctuations created a generation of both myth and nightmare in the crypto world. The BitMEX Liquidation Engine was once the most feared force in the market—when a large number of long and short positions were liquidated simultaneously, the entire market price would experience a waterfall-like chain reaction within minutes. This unique market phenomenon even spurred the development of third-party tools specifically for tracking BitMEX liquidation data. II. Its Place in Crypto History: What Did It Change? Perpetual Contracts Become the Industry Standard BitMEX's perpetual contracts have now been copied by all major crypto exchanges—Binance, Bybit, OKX, dYdX, Hyperliquid… Every major derivatives platform is essentially running a product variant based on BitMEX's original design. It can be said that today's entire crypto derivatives market—worth trillions of dollars—is built on the product prototype established by BitMEX. The "312" network outage prevented BTC from continuing its plunge. Impacted by the massive outbreak of the COVID-19 pandemic in Europe and the United States, on March 12, 2020, cryptocurrencies, led by Bitcoin, suddenly and unexpectedly experienced a sharp drop. The price of Bitcoin (BTC) began to fall from around $7,000, plummeting 50% in 24 hours, briefly dipping below $3,800. BitMEX, the trading platform with the largest short positions at the time, suspended its trading function on the morning of March 13th, a move commonly known as "pulling the plug." This action prevented BTC from continuing its downward spiral. To some extent, this saved the entire crypto industry. It defined the price discovery mechanism for "on-chain Bitcoin." During BitMEX's heyday from 2017 to 2019, Bitcoin price discovery primarily occurred in BitMEX's perpetual contract market, not the spot market. BitMEX's contract prices were once the most important benchmark for global market makers. This influence is almost unimaginable today—at that time, the entire industry's liquidity was so concentrated in one place. BitMEX became the first cryptocurrency exchange to be sued by the DOJ/CFTC, reshaping compliance awareness. This historic event had a profound impact on the entire industry. It sent a clear signal to all offshore exchanges that even if registered overseas, accepting US users puts them under the scrutiny of US regulators. In the two years following BitMEX's lawsuit, major exchanges such as Binance, Kraken, and Coinbase significantly increased their investment in KYC/AML compliance, and the "sword of Damocles" of regulation has since hung over the entire industry. BitMEX boasts an impeccable security record. Throughout its more than 11 years of operation, BitMEX has maintained an outstanding security record. Unlike many of its peers, BitMEX achieved zero user fund losses due to hacking attacks, no smart contract vulnerabilities, and no internal theft during its entire operation—a truly rare achievement.

Against the backdrop of frequent industry scandals, exchange thefts, and crashes (Mt.Gox, FTX), this record is all the more valuable.

III. Why the Closure: Five Layers of Structural Failure

The closure of BitMEX, ostensibly the result of a "strategic review," was in reality the accumulation of five layers of structural failure over a decade.

First Layer: The 2020 Blow—The DOJ/CFTC Lawsuit Changed Everything

In October 2020, the U.S. Department of Justice and the CFTC filed a lawsuit against BitMEX and its executives, accusing them of failing to establish adequate anti-money laundering measures. The company and its founders later pleaded guilty to the charges.

Hayes, Delo, and Reed resigned shortly after the U.S. criminal charges were filed.

This was not just a legal event, but a sudden collapse of market confidence.

Within 48 hours of the lawsuit announcement, over 45,000 bitcoins were withdrawn from BitMEX and flowed to other exchanges. At the price levels at the time, this meant a loss of over $500 million in assets within two days, a direct expression of user distrust. BitMEX ultimately paid a $100 million fine to the CFTC, and HDR Global Trading Limited was ordered by the court to pay the same amount in January 2025. Notably, this entity received a corporate pardon from the Trump administration on March 28, 2025, reportedly the first presidential pardon for a company in US history, and was pardoned before the $100 million fine payment deadline. The second layer: A fundamental reversal of the competitive landscape. BitMEX held a 36.9% share of the Bitcoin futures market at the beginning of 2020, but by October of that year, after the lawsuit, its market share had plummeted to 16.8%. Where did this fleeing liquidity go? The majority of the funds flowed to Binance and Bybit, both of which almost completely replicated BitMEX's perpetual contract model in terms of product, but offered platforms with higher liquidity, lower fees, and a better user experience. BitMEX invented the product, but failed to maintain the market share it generated. Latest data shows that BitMEX's daily trading volume hovers around $580 million, with an annualized value of approximately $211 billion. This represents a drop of about 80% compared to its 2019 peak. The third layer: Uninterested attempts to sell. BitMEX hired Broadhaven Capital Partners to lead the sale process as early as February 2025, but ultimately failed to find a buyer to complete the transaction. This detail is extremely crucial. It means that under a formal sale process led by professional M&A advisors, no institution was willing to take over an exchange with a complex legal history and shrinking market share at a reasonable price. Closure was the only way out after the failed sale.

Fourth Layer: Systemic Loss of Leadership

Just three weeks before the closure announcement, BitMEX had just lost its CEO, CFO, and Head of Growth.

The collective departure of the core management team before the company's closure is a classic sign of an institution nearing its end.

Executives knew the outcome three weeks before the announcement.

Fifth Layer: The Era Has Turned

Perhaps this is the most fundamental reason.

When BitMEX was founded in 2014, the crypto market lacked any professional derivatives infrastructure; BitMEX was a pioneer in filling that gap.

By 2026, that gap has long been filled.

Binance handles dozens of times the derivatives trading volume of BitMEX, and Hyperliquid is gaining market share at an astonishing rate in the on-chain perpetual contract field (OI has exceeded $11 billion).

Every competitive advantage BitMEX once possessed—high leverage, offshore registration, and no KYC (note: BitMEX only began implementing KYC after being sued by the US in 2020)—has either become industry standard or a regulatory burden by 2026. It wasn't wrong; it was simply caught up with the times. IV. An Orderly Closing Down The closure timeline has a clear phased arrangement. From August 26, 2026, at 04:00 UTC, the platform will enter a "reduction-only" mode, prohibiting the opening of new positions and only allowing position reduction operations; when it finally closes on September 23, all remaining open positions will be forcibly liquidated. Users who have completed KYC verification but still have assets after the closure date will be charged a monthly account management fee of $50 or 1% of the annualized balance (whichever is higher). BitMEX has confirmed that all previously staked BMEX tokens have been unstaking and returned to user accounts. The company's current proof-of-reserve shows that the platform's liabilities are fully covered by customer assets. Conclusion: The Fate of an "Inventor" There's a cruel rule in the crypto industry: the inventor isn't necessarily the ultimate winner. BitMEX invented perpetual contracts, defined the basic paradigm for crypto derivatives, and at its peak controlled 57% of the global related market. But its legacy ultimately continues in a unique way. Not on its own platform, but on thousands of competitors who adopted its product design. The most representative of these is Hyperliquid, which is rapidly rising today. "When BitMEX was founded in 2014, our mission was simple: to provide everyone with professional-grade access to crypto derivatives." It has fulfilled this mission. It changed the way the world trades crypto assets, and was then surpassed by the world it changed. On September 23, 2026, at 04:00 UTC, an era officially ended.

data.title: Circle Teams Up With Kakao And Toss Bank To Build Stablecoin Payment Infrastructure In South Korea
data.description:
Circle Expands South Korea Push With Kakao Group And Toss Bank Stablecoin Partnerships

Circle is deepening its presence in South Korea by teaming up with two of the country's biggest financial technology groups, as interest in stablecoins grows ahead of new digital asset rules.

The stablecoin issuer has signed separate memorandums of understanding (MOUs) with Kakao Group and Toss Bank to explore blockchain-powered payment infrastructure, digital asset services and cross-border transactions.

The collaborations build on Circle's wider strategy in South Korea after partnering with the country's two largest crypto exchanges, Upbit and Bithumb, in April.

Circle 🤝 Kakao Group

Circle and Kakao Group have signed an MOU to explore blockchain-based payment infrastructure and digital asset technologies in Korea.

Together, we’ll assess opportunities for USDC and Circle’s global payment rails across payments, settlement, and digital… pic.twitter.com/MmZRd19iIH

— Circle (@circle) July 23, 2026

Kakao And Circle Explore Stablecoin Payment Network

Circle will work with Kakao, Kakao Pay and Kakao Bank to study how its blockchain technology and global payment infrastructure can connect with Kakao's digital platforms and financial services.

The companies plan to examine stablecoin payments, cross-border remittances, merchant settlements and ways to link blockchain networks with existing financial systems.

They will also assess opportunities in tokenised financial services, although no products or launch timeline have been announced.

Kakao Group is also preparing to build a won-backed stablecoin ecosystem by combining the payment capabilities of Kakao Pay and the banking services of Kakao Bank through its KakaoTalk platform.

The company said the infrastructure could eventually support stablecoin services developed by other South Korean businesses.

Quite an interesting piece for me, probably because I'm not a Korean (never been there before) and only learned that Kakao Group has so many branches.

Why Kakao's signal into the stablecoin race is a big deal? Here's what I knew:

Kakao's fully integrated ecosystem is seen as… https://t.co/2aVUEuT9a7

— June (@junebananana) August 8, 2025

Shin Won-geun, Chief Executive Officer of Kakao Pay and head of Kakao Group's stablecoin task force, said,

"Based on our platform, payment, and financial service experiences accumulated in everyday life, we will proactively prepare a Korean-style digital asset ecosystem with Circle and set new standards for future financial infrastructure."

Before announcing the agreement, Circle executives met Kakao representatives in Pangyo on 22 July.

Toss Bank Studies USDC And Blockchain Payments

Circle also signed a separate MOU with Viva Republica, the operator of Toss, and Toss Bank to explore stablecoin-based financial services.

The partnership will assess digital wallets, overseas payments, blockchain settlement and programmable on-chain transactions powered by USDC.

Circle 🤝 @toss__official

Circle is collaborating with Toss and Toss Bank to explore blockchain-based payment infrastructure and stablecoin technology in Korea.

Together, we’ll assess stablecoin-based payment opportunities across one of Korea’s most widely used fintech… pic.twitter.com/JLdsenZQJ6

— Circle (@circle) July 23, 2026

Toss will also review biometric payment technology and USDC-linked financial products, while Toss Bank will focus on connecting stablecoin infrastructure with traditional bank accounts and existing payment networks.

The companies also plan to evaluate compliance, security, risk management and anti-money laundering measures as South Korea continues developing its digital asset regulations.

The latest agreement follows Toss Bank's partnership with the Solana Foundation last month to develop blockchain-based financial infrastructure for global users.

Circle Builds On Earlier South Korea Deals

The new partnerships extend Circle's expansion in the country following its agreements with Upbit and Bithumb in April.

The two exchanges frequently account for more than 95% of South Korea's daily cryptocurrency trading volume.

At the time, Circle Chief Executive Officer Jeremy Allaire said the partnerships "span continued work in the promotion and adoption of USDC on Korean exchanges and other technology collaborations that we can pursue around some of the other things that Circle is working on."

Just wrapped an incredible few days in Seoul. South Korea is moving fast on stablecoin and digital asset regulation — and the ambition here to do things right is real.

Met with an extraordinary range of leaders: superapps like KakaoGroup, leading crypto exchanges, banks,… pic.twitter.com/QIDFHkXPdQ

— Jeremy Allaire - jerallaire.arc (@jerallaire) April 15, 2026

Why Stablecoins Are Drawing Attention In South Korea

South Korea is working on legislation to regulate won-backed stablecoins as policymakers seek to encourage digital payment innovation while introducing safeguards for reserves, redemption and issuer oversight.

Proposed rules are expected to cover stablecoin issuance, collateral management and internal controls.

However, lawmakers and regulators remain divided over which organisations should be allowed to issue won-backed stablecoins.

The Bank of Korea has argued that banks should retain a majority stake in stablecoin issuers, while the Financial Services Commission has warned that limiting eligibility could reduce competition and slow innovation.

The government also listed progress on the Digital Asset Basic Act among its priorities for the second half of 2026 in its economic growth strategy released on 14 July.

Meanwhile, financial institutions have continued testing blockchain technology ahead of the new framework.

Internet-only lender Kbank partnered with Ripple in April to test blockchain-based remittances, while KB Financial Group completed a pilot in May covering stablecoin issuance, offline merchant payments and cross-border remittances on the Kaia blockchain.

The group said it is preparing to introduce stablecoin services once the regulations take effect.

data.title: OpenAI's model went out of control; Zhipu came to the rescue. The US-China AI competition turned into a Star Wars scenario.
data.description:
Tao Zhu, Jinse Finance

Abstract:The global AI competition in 2026 is increasingly resembling a real-life Star Wars story. On one side are closed-source AI from the US, represented by OpenAI and Anthropic; on the other are open-source AI from China, represented by DeepSeek and Zhipu. China's open-source power is driving a transformation in the AI ​​industry through lower-cost and more open methods.

I. OpenAI Model Testing Invades Hugging Face, China's Zhipu Takes Emergency Action

Previously, the market generally believed that the AI ​​competition between China and the US was still at the level of model performance. However, the recent incident of OpenAI models unexpectedly "going out of control" and invading Hugging Face, with China's Zhipu GLM-5.2 assisting in completing the defense analysis, has proven that this model performance competition has long since escalated into a full-scale war of security, open source, and rules.

Last week, OpenAI's artificial intelligence models infiltrated the internal systems of AI startup Hugging Face. These models carried out the intrusion within just a few hours.

According to OpenAI's official website, "The model identified and exploited vulnerabilities in OpenAI's research environment and Hugging Face's production infrastructure, directly obtaining test results from Hugging Face's production database." A skilled hacker would typically need weeks to accomplish the same task. An informed source indicated that OpenAI has been in contact with the US government since learning of the intrusion. An OpenAI spokesperson stated that the company has communicated with law enforcement and other government agencies regarding the matter and has transparently informed them of the investigation results. The accidental intrusion of OpenAI models into Hugging Face involved three OpenAI models—GPT-5.6 Sol and two other undisclosed models—which worked together to discover and exploit a series of vulnerabilities, leading to the intrusion. This incident exposes a problem: when AI possesses autonomous planning, tool access, internet access, and long-term execution capabilities, it is fundamentally different from traditional software. The risks of AI in the future will no longer be limited to generating incorrect answers, but could potentially act like real hackers. OpenAI stated in its blog post: "Artificial intelligence is accelerating the discovery and exploitation of vulnerabilities. The main lesson from this incident is that model security must keep pace with rapidly evolving capabilities. We are strengthening the containment, monitoring, access control, and assessment measures used in the model development process." Hugging Face co-founder and CEO Clément Delangue noted: "We have been working closely with the @OpenAI team for the past 24 hours (thank you so much!), and we firmly believe they have no malicious intent. It's incredible that this all happened autonomously!" Even more dramatically, after the incident, cybersecurity teams needed AI to assist in analyzing the attack chain, but mainstream US models malfunctioned—due to OpenAI and Anthropic's long-standing strict security policies, they struggled to distinguish between defensive security research and offensive hacking activities. According to Reuters, these models either restricted access to their most advanced models or, for security reasons, were designed to reject tasks related to hacking attacks. For example, Anthropic's advanced model, Claude Fable 5, routes cybersecurity queries to an older model, while OpenAI's GPT-5.6 Sol has protection mechanisms designed to prevent cyberattacks. Therefore, researchers turned their attention to the Chinese open-source model, GLM-5.2. GLM-5.2 features: a 1MB ultra-long context; multi-tool invocation capabilities; long-chain task execution capabilities; and agent engineering capabilities. Cybersecurity company Semgrep claims that in some benchmark tests, GLM-5.2 outperforms Anthropic's Claude Opus 4.8 model released in May. Researchers stated that with further instructions, Opus 4.8 and GLM-5.2 are comparable to Mythos in vulnerability finding capabilities. It is evident that the advantages of Chinese AI models have extended from price to performance capabilities. II. Chinese AI Models Spark Discussion Within the US On July 20th, according to Axios, citing sources familiar with the matter, the US government is considering banning Chinese artificial intelligence models. On July 23, U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent posted on the X platform, stating, "We support open-source AI and the innovation it brings, but open source does not mean we can arbitrarily infringe on U.S. intellectual property. When Chinese companies engage in model distillation in a covert and large-scale manner, crossing the line and constituting intellectual property theft, the United States will consider taking measures such as sanctions and adding the relevant companies to the Entity List." The "Chinese companies engaging in model distillation in a covert and large-scale manner" that Bessent referred to is the U.S.'s claim that Kimi K3 distilled the Fable 5 model. Michael Kratsios, Trump's chief science advisor and director of the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy, stated, "Our information indicates that Dark Side of the Moon 'distilled' Anthropic's Fable 5 model when developing its Kimi K3 model." He also accused Dark Side of using chips from Nvidia. (Note: The US believes Dark Side obtained Nvidia GB300 servers, which are banned from sale to China, through intermediaries in Thailand.) Under this post, netizens offered sharp comments: @kristoph: You've been misled. Kimi 3 was released just one month after Fable. It's impossible for them to distill and train that model in such a short time. @OopsGuess: You mean Huawei "stole" 5G that the US never had? Now Chinese open-source AI is supposedly beating top-tier US cutting-edge models through "distillation"? That's amazing. The US keeps accusing China of stealing things it can't manufacture, can't price, can't open-source, and can't compete with. This isn't an intellectual property case; it's just that the US can't accept defeat. Time and time again. If Washington can't accept defeat, just say so. If it's desperate enough to use sanctions to stop China's progress, then be brave and honest. There's no need to package insecurity as intellectual property law. Since Washington suddenly likes to talk about stolen data, let's start with the licensed training data from OpenAI and Anthropic.Produce the evidence.

  • @awesome_fingers: Can you stop Kimi from releasing the weights as open source? If not, then shut up! Maybe go and ban Fable again. That would make you feel better.

  • @michaelgrowth: 1. Anthropic extracted the entire internet and creators without paying them. Where were you then? 2. K3 predates Fable, so that part doesn't hold water. 3. It's nearly impossible to extract, train, test, and deploy a model of this scale in just a few weeks. 4. Stop copying your idiotic political colleagues. The whole world can see how fucking stupid you are and how easily you can be manipulated. Other U.S. officials have also made statements advocating for banning Chinese AI. For example, in January 2025, Missouri Republican Senator Josh Hawley introduced the "Decoupling U.S. Artificial Intelligence Capabilities from China Act," aimed at severing U.S.-China cooperation in the field of artificial intelligence. This bill would prohibit the import and export of AI technology from China, ban U.S. companies from conducting related research in China, and prohibit any U.S. investment in Chinese AI technology companies. Hawley stated, "Every dollar and every GB of data flowing into Chinese AI will ultimately be used against the United States. The United States cannot tolerate our biggest adversary gaining such power." Three months ago, Stanford's 2026 AI Index report indicated that as of March 2026, the top U.S. model, Claude Opus 4.6, had an Elo score of 1503, while the top Chinese model closely followed, with a difference of only 2.7%. The United States still maintains its lead in basic model innovation, capital investment, and computing infrastructure. The United States has 5,427 data centers, more than 10 times that of any other country, and its energy consumption is also the highest in the world. In contrast, China excels in both scientific research output and industrial application. China leads in the number of published papers, citations, and patents, and ranks among the world's top in the deployment of industrial robots (physical AI field), accounting for 54% of global installations. Clearly, Chinese AI is rapidly approaching the level of American AI, creating a significant sense of urgency for the United States. However, Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang points out that the US should not fear China's open-source AI models, but rather the growing movement to ban them. "These Chinese models are excellent, and excellent open-source models should be used. We will always use artificial intelligence. The US will exist for a long time, and China will exist for a long time." III. Liang Wenfeng: Even the Strongest Models Will Be Open Source While major US model companies continue their closed-source strategies and US officials are still debating whether to ban Chinese large-scale models, Liang Wenfeng has already publicly expressed his initial intention and vision for open source. On May 20th, DeepSeek founder Liang Wenfeng held a 4-hour meeting with investors. Liang Wenfeng pointed out: "I think we will open-source, and our strongest model will likely also be open-source. Because I don't see any benefits in keeping it closed-source, I don't see any inherent advantages. Even if the model is open-source, telling others everything is a very high barrier to entry. For others to use it, that barrier is also very high. It's difficult for them to use it; secondly, for them to use it, they also need to keep the cost very low, which is also very, very difficult, not that easy." "OpenAI initially thought it could really monopolize the world, but in reality, it will encounter many, many challengers… For us, we don't aim to make the most profit, or calculate the maximum return on investment, but only to earn a reasonable return." For DeepSeek, success doesn't lie in having more money, but in training world-class large-scale models at low cost.

    Liang Wenfeng's statement stands in stark contrast to OpenAI's development path in recent years.

    When OpenAI was founded in 2015, it publicly declared its ambition to establish an open, non-profit artificial intelligence research institution, allowing AI achievements to benefit all of humanity. However, starting with GPT-3, OpenAI gradually shifted to a closed-source approach, no longer publicly disclosing model weights, but instead providing commercial services through APIs and ChatGPT; subsequently, it attracted hundreds of billions of dollars in investment from Microsoft and gradually formed a business model centered on models, APIs, and enterprise services.

    DeepSeek, on the other hand, has chosen a completely different path. From DeepSeek-R1 to subsequent models, the company has continuously disclosed model weights, training details, etc., hoping to attract global developers to participate in ecosystem building.

    OpenAI represents the closed-source commercialization route, while DeepSeek represents the open-source inclusive route, and the US government is attempting to slow down the development of AI in China through regulation, export controls, and technology restrictions. But clearly, the speed of AI development in China is rising stronger amidst this suppression.

    A true AI-powered version of "Star Wars" has begun.

data.title: BitMEX To Shut Down After 11 Years, Closing The Chapter On A Crypto Trading Pioneer
data.description:
The Godfather of crypto derivatives Closes Its Doors, leaving a lasting legacy

BitMEX, one of the exchanges that helped shape the modern cryptocurrency derivatives market, will shut down later this year after owner HDR Global Trading Limited decided to wind down the business following a strategic review.

This is precisely why the DTCC’s participation in the tokenization race is significant.🙇‍♂️

Derivatives.🔥

The phase of crypto adoption where investors actually earn profits.💸

Listen.👂 https://t.co/J2QlJVSdmf

— SMQKE (@SMQKEDQG) July 22, 2026

The exchange announced Thursday that it will cease operations on Sept. 23, 2026, bringing to an end an 11-year run that saw BitMEX emerge as one of the industry's most influential trading platforms during crypto's early growth.

THE BLOCK: BitMEX, the exchange Arthur Hayes co-founded in 2014, will shut down Sept. 23 following a "strategic review" by platform operator HDR Global Trading.

New registrations are already halted. Risk limits kick in Aug. 26, and any positions still open at closure get… pic.twitter.com/KkEOyKpsQ0

— The Block (@TheBlockCo) July 23, 2026

Users have been advised to close open positions and withdraw their assets before the shutdown. BitMEX said customer funds remain secure throughout the transition, adding that assets are "fully safe and under your control" while operations are wound down.

While BitMEX's market influence has faded in recent years, its impact on the crypto industry remains significant.

The exchange played a pivotal role in popularizing perpetual swap contracts — a derivatives product that allows traders to speculate on cryptocurrency prices without an expiration date. The instrument has since become the backbone of crypto derivatives trading and is now offered by nearly every major digital asset exchange.

🚨 BitMEX is officially shutting down all operations on September 23, 2026.

The platform that invented the 100x perpetual swap and defined modern crypto leverage trading is closing its doors after 11 years.

As liquidity shifts entirely toward regulated institutional channels,… pic.twitter.com/Htp8O2ksfP

— That Martini Guy ₿ (@MartiniGuyYT) July 23, 2026

BitMEX also pointed to its security record, saying it operated throughout its 11-year history without suffering a hack that resulted in customer fund losses.

The closure marks the end of one of crypto's earliest trading pioneers, whose innovations helped define today's derivatives market even as larger global exchanges came to dominate the sector.

data.title: Co-evolution: A Forecast of Ten Major Trends in Artificial Intelligence in 2026
data.description: In 2026, the AI ​​industry is undergoing a profound paradigm shift, moving from pursuing model scale to pursuing intelligent usability. The evolution of basic models is no longer limited to the training phase; engineering deployment has become the new competitive frontier, and business and organizational restructuring is also progressing simultaneously. We have outlined ten key trends across three main areas: model evolution, engineering infrastructure, and business and organization. These ten trends constitute an interconnected systemic evolution: the evolutionary methods are changing, the deployment methods are changing, and the resulting human-machine relationship is also changing. Technical Capabilities: What is AI Evolving? (I) Online Evolution: Evolution Goes Beyond Training, Models Continue to Grow After Deployment The most noteworthy change in the basic model in 2026 is that the timing of evolution has extended from the training phase to after deployment. This is manifested in three simultaneously advancing curves. The first is the diffusion of reinforcement learning from code and mathematics to more verifiable domains. The current obstacle to diffusion lies in domain data infrastructure: truly valuable scientific data remains locked in national laboratories and academic institutions. Whoever establishes a pathway for programmatic access first will gain entry into this wave of diffusion. The second key aspect is that models become increasingly adept at understanding scenarios with use, which is the most noteworthy direction among the three trends. Current cutting-edge large models are not adept at learning from context; even when information is readily available, the most powerful models only achieve a 17% task completion rate. The key theme for 2026 is Context Learning, with the broader battleground being Memory Consolidation—ensuring that what models learn can be retained across sessions. Determining which information is worth retaining across sessions, how to retrieve it, and how to prevent interference—these engineering questions will become the most practical product moat in 2026. In terms of user experience, AI is transforming from an extremely intelligent stranger who doesn't recognize you into a work partner that understands you better the more you use it. The third point is that AI accelerates AI research itself. Anthropic disclosed that over 80% of its code merged into the production environment was written by Claude, and its ability to optimize training code has jumped from 3 times to 52 times, achieving an order-of-magnitude leap within a year. Currently, the core bottleneck lies in sustained autonomy rather than intelligence: the time it takes for AI to reliably complete tasks doubles every four months, reaching the 4-minute level in 2024 and the 16-hour level in 2026. While it's still some distance from achieving weeks of autonomous research, the direction is clear. (II) Multimodal Cognition: From Renderer to Creator, Multimodal AI Begins to Understand the World The core change in multimodal AI in 2026 is the evolution of generation behavior from a one-time output to a multi-step decision-making process. The usability rate of cutting-edge models for 15-second videos has increased from approximately 20% to around 90%, crossing the critical point for entering the production process. Achieving controllability is a prerequisite for agentification, and this breakthrough is of great significance. At the architectural level, native multimodality is replacing the splicing mode of language model scheduling plus visual model execution. Images and text engage in token-level cross-modal attention interactions within a shared latent space, giving rise to preliminary visual reasoning capabilities. It's worth noting that the focus of competition in world models has shifted from predicting the next frame to predicting the next state after an action, i.e., evolving from renderers to planners. The key judgment is: understanding the world doesn't necessarily require reconstructing it. Architectures that make predictions in abstract space rather than pixel-by-pixel reconstruction can achieve zero-sample control of the physical environment with a small amount of unlabeled data, meaning a more efficient path for scenarios requiring real-time planning, such as robotics and autonomous driving. Whoever upgrades their world model from a perception tool to a planning engine first will gain a more advantageous starting position in the next generation of embodied intelligence competition. (III) Scientific Intelligence: A Three-in-One Approach – The Battle for AI for Science Begins. The AI-driven research paradigm, integrating foundational models, intelligent research agents, and autonomous laboratories, is expected to transition from the exploratory phase to a more mature stage by 2026. Google, Anthropic, and the Shanghai AI Lab in China, among others, are all using large-scale foundational models as their core, upgrading research AI from single-point tools to systemic platforms. The commercialization of the Autonomous Laboratory (SDL) is beginning to materialize. XtalPi generates over 50,000 reaction yield data points monthly and has achieved its first annual profit; globally, approximately 170 AI-discovered or optimized drug projects have entered clinical development, with several projects entering Phase III trials. However, it is noteworthy that the faster technological progress, the more crucial trusted infrastructure becomes, a key constraint on the sustainable advancement of scientific intelligence. Harness Engineering: How Humans Harness This Evolution (IV) Harness Engineering: Putting Harnesses on Wild Horses and Moving the Frontier of Competition Outside the Model (By 2026, the core work of AI engineers will have shifted from how to ask the model questions to how to design the model's operating environment, i.e., Harness Engineering) Anthropic and Cursor published articles and defined this direction through practice, providing models with an operational framework for memory, context, tool calls, multi-step planning, and failure recovery. Practice came first, then terminology. This matter became crucial in 2026, stemming from the simultaneous pressure from two directions. The marginal benefit of simply increasing pre-training is diminishing. Inference-time compute (ITC) has become a new focus, allowing the model to perform multiple rounds of self-validation during runtime, which improves performance on complex tasks. Harness is responsible for orchestrating these steps. The difference in reality is already evident: for the same GPT-4o model, teams with well-built Harness can run autonomous processes of more than six steps, while teams without Harness remain stuck at a single question-and-answer session. Cognition's Devin achieves 13.86% on software engineering benchmarks, while the bare model achieves less than 2% on the same task. The essence of the difference lies in the engineering quality of the runtime environment. In the long run, Harness has a predictable end result: the model will gradually internalize it. Today, the ability to build externally is needed, and each generation of models is incorporating it. The current window of explicitness means that a batch of scattered engineering practices are being cataloged into design patterns. For example, ADPS (Agent Design Pattern Specification) has compiled 28 standardized agent building routines, and a selection manual has been published. Standardization means this window of opportunity won't last long. (V) Construction by All: From Coding to Full-Time Work, the Right to Construct is Handed Over to Ordinary People for the First Time. AI programming is the first battleground of this wave of Agents, and the debate over whether AI can independently complete software delivery will basically end in 2026. On the SWE-bench Verified benchmark, code fix capability jumped 38 percentage points in 22 months, from 49% for Claude 3.5 Sonnet (June 2024) to 87.6% for Opus 4.7 (April 2026). 95% of the code on Anthropic's Claude Code product team is written in Claude Code (a more refined AI-native development product), and over 90% of Tencent's engineers use CodeBuddy, with AI-generated code accounting for more than half. A more profound change lies in the aspect of accessibility: for the first time, building software no longer requires coding skills. Six to seven out of ten Bolt users are designers, students, and salespeople, not programmers. The barrier hasn't disappeared, it has simply shifted; the prototype wall has become the new watershed. AI has reduced the time from zero to prototype to almost zero, but a chasm exists between prototype and operational capability: GitClear analysis shows that technical debt increases by 30-40% after large-scale AI intervention, and Veracode found that 45% of AI coding tasks introduce known security vulnerabilities. Therefore, the truly scarce skill has evolved from simply knowing how to write code to knowing how to make judgments. This set of capabilities is accelerating the crossing of code boundaries. In the first half of 2026, Anthropic's Claude Cowork, OpenAI's upgraded Codex, and Tencent's WorkBuddy emerged, with leading programming products collectively evolving into general-purpose work agents. Programming is by far the most demanding scenario for agents, and the execution kernels tested here have performed excellently when transferred to knowledge work such as writing reports and performing data analysis, with the generalization trend continuing to advance. (VI) Trusted Execution: Laying the Foundation of Trust, From Autonomy to Trust As AI agents move towards autonomous execution, security risks are simultaneously amplified, and traditional security and approval models have become largely ineffective in the face of machine-level speeds. A series of landmark events occurred in 2026: Microsoft 365 Copilot was found to have a zero-click hint injection vulnerability (CVE-2025-32711); Anthropic disclosed the first almost fully autonomous AI-driven cyberattack; and the DeFi platform Step Finance suffered a loss of approximately $30 million due to excessive privileges granted to its agents, leading to induced transfers. To enable AI to move from autonomous execution to trusted execution, three things must be managed effectively: verifiable identity, traceable behavior, and minimized operational privileges, throughout the entire lifecycle of the agent. Security initiatives are being implemented in three directions: First, communication protocols are moving from fragmentation to unification. Google donated its A2A protocol to the Linux Foundation and released A2A v1.0, mandating mTLS two-way authentication. Second, intelligent agents are obtaining unique and verifiable identities. TC260 proposed requirements for intelligent agent identity identification and traceability in 2026. Third, compliance supervision is shifting from static auditing to dynamic supervision covering the entire process. China jointly issued the "Implementation Opinions on the Standardized Application and Innovative Development of Intelligent Agents" in May 2026. Business and Organization: How Evolution Reshapes Human Society (VII) Intelligence as a Service: Tokens Retreat to the Background, What's Sold Is No Longer Raw Materials The Big Model is Loosening the Constraint that Intelligence Is Difficult to Trade in the Market. In the past, companies mainly acquired intelligence by hiring people, buying software, and outsourcing. With the emergence of the Big Model, a fourth form has taken shape: directly purchasing intelligence services packaged from models, tools, data, and workflows, including customer service agents, contract review processes, AI sales representatives, etc. Tokens are units of measurement, but they cannot carry value. The same 10,000 tokens might only be used to refine a single sentence, or they might help identify key risks in contracts worth millions. This has led to a hybrid market structure: cost is calculated using tokens and computing power, while pricing is differentiated by process, outcome, and job capabilities, varying according to scenario and willingness to pay. Pricing models are shifting further to the right along the spectrum from selling resources to selling jobs, with the seller's responsibility increasing progressively. 11x.ai prices its AI sales representatives at 40-50% of the total cost of a human SDR, while Intercom Fin charges based on resolving a single customer service order. The shift in transaction objects from consumption to delivery signifies that AI commercialization has entered a mature stage. (VIII) The Internet of Things: The New Subject of the Internet, When Agents Start Going Online Starting in 2025, agents will join the Internet as independent actors, forming a new subject. They search for information, book services, call APIs, and collaborate with other agents without opening apps, browsing news feeds, or watching ads. The exposure foundation of the attention economy is losing its ground. The era of daily active users (DAU) and time spent as core product metrics is crumbling. Task completion rate (TCR) is replacing DAU as the North Star metric for the next generation of Internet products. The competitive logic of the internet is being rewritten simultaneously: from traffic distribution to capability scheduling. Whoever can understand user intent, organize multiple capabilities, and complete complex tasks will hold a key position in the next-generation platform ecosystem. The competitive barrier for vertical agents lies not in the basic model, but in industry depth: industry data accumulation, depth of integration with business systems, and depth of understanding of industry workflows. These three thresholds will determine who can truly embed themselves into core business processes. IDC data shows that over 45% of enterprises globally have deployed AI agents with autonomous decision-making capabilities in their core businesses, with an average ROI of 171%. (ix) Liquid Organization: Organizations begin to flow, moving from solid structures to liquid orchestration. The organizational restructuring in the tech industry in 2026 follows the same logic: replacing the information routing function of middle management with AI, and reconstructing organizations into operating systems based on intelligent orchestration. After laying off 40% of its staff, Block's founder published an article titled "From Hierarchy to Intelligence." Meta laid off 8,000 people while transferring 7,000 to four new AI departments. Anthropic's annualized revenue is $14 billion, while its growth team consists of only about 40 people. Large companies reducing redundancy and small teams releasing capacity point in the same direction: labor is no longer confined to positions, and teams are no longer confined to departments. The co-creation of humans and AI agents has become the new normal in teams. A typical setup involves 2 to 5 people supervising 50 to 100 specialized agents, taking over end-to-end processes that previously required larger teams. The role of humans has shifted from operators of every task to managers of agent teams and problem definers; effective judgment is becoming a new scarcity within organizations. This increased organizational flexibility is accompanied by a rewriting of employment contracts. Individualized corporatization (The proportion of new companies founded by a single person in the United States will increase from 23.7% in 2019 to 36.3% in 2025) , organizational partnerships, and contribution-based pricing replacing time-based pay are three trends advancing simultaneously. After the old hierarchical order was broken up, the new operating rules have not yet emerged, which is the core tension facing current organizational transformation. (X) Role Restructuring: From Supervisor to Architect, the Career Focus is Shifting Upwards AI restructures tasks, not positions, and its impact is on the granularity of tasks. The Anthropic Economic Index analysis shows that in approximately 36% of occupations, at least a quarter of the tasks have already involved AI; however, in only about 4% of occupations, more than three-quarters of the tasks are covered by AI. AI first enters certain tasks within a job, rather than replacing the entire job all at once. Once AI agents possess continuous execution capabilities, each employee begins managing a group of digital workers. Microsoft's Work Trend Index proposes "Every employee becomes an agent boss," which will be quite concrete in the 2026 work scenario: researchers have one agent scanning papers daily, another agent organizing data, and another agent writing presentations; each employee is managing a group of digital workers. Architectural capabilities have thus diverged from individual paths into six organizational tasks: AI workflow architecture, agent orchestration, context engineering, AI assessment and trust management, AI ROI measurement, and continuous learning operations. Individual employees using AI can bring localized efficiency improvements; whether an organization can leverage AI as a structural advantage depends on its ability to systematically configure people and agents. Microsoft's proposed human-agent ratio is becoming a real management variable. The main theme of the 2026 AI career map can be summarized as follows: execution capabilities are becoming cheaper, while architectural capabilities are becoming more expensive. In summary, the ten trends mentioned above all point to one conclusion: the era of co-evolution of AI has arrived. Evolution is no longer confined to the training phase in laboratories; it is extending to every stage of deployment, use, organization, and business. The core challenge facing technology companies has shifted from how to choose better base models to how to co-evolve with AI at the entire system level.

data.title: Crypto Exchange BitMEX Confirms Shutdown After 11 Years, Urges Traders to Close Positions Before September Deadline
data.description:
BitMEX to End 11-Year Run as Crypto Derivatives Exchange Shuts Down

BitMEX, one of the exchanges that helped build today's crypto derivatives market, is preparing to close after more than a decade in operation.

Parent company HDR Global Trading Limited has decided to wind down the business following a strategic review, bringing an end to a platform that introduced 100x leveraged perpetual swaps and became a favourite among professional traders.

The exchange will permanently cease operations at 04:00 UTC on 23 September 2026.

Existing customers have been urged to close open positions and withdraw their assets during the transition, while new account registrations have already been suspended.

BitMEX to shut down operations on September 23, 2026 at 04:00 UTC.

After an 11-year run pioneering 100x perpetual swaps, the exchange is closing following a strategic review by parent company HDR Global Trading Limited. New registrations stopped immediately; users should close… pic.twitter.com/HWxXZoxBOi

— Coinlive (@CoinliveHQ) July 23, 2026

BitMEX Sets Final Closure After Strategic Review

HDR Global Trading Limited said the decision followed a review of both the company and the broader cryptocurrency industry.

While the exchange did not give a detailed reason for the closure, it confirmed the shutdown is not linked to a security breach, insolvency or missing customer funds.

In a message to users, BitMEX sought to reassure customers throughout the wind-down process.

“We want to reassure you that your assets remain fully safe and under your control during this transition period.”

The company also said its reserves continue to exceed customer liabilities and pointed users to its Proof of Reserves and Liabilities page to demonstrate that customer assets remain fully backed.

Dear BitMEX Users,

Today, we share with a very heavy heart that BitMEX exchange will shut down its operations, effective 23 September 2026 at 04:00:00 UTC.

The owner and operator of BitMEX, HDR Global Trading Limited, has made the difficult decision to close operations… pic.twitter.com/oWuqlh547f

— BitMEX (@BitMEX) July 23, 2026

August Exit Deadline Matters More Than September

Although the exchange will officially close on 23 September 2026, the more important deadline for traders arrives nearly a month earlier.

From 04:00 UTC on 26 August 2026, users will no longer be able to open new positions or increase existing ones.

Only position reductions will be permitted as BitMEX begins an orderly wind-down of its markets.

🚨 BREAKING: BitMEX, co-founded by Arthur Hayes and the pioneer of crypto perpetual swaps, will permanently shut down on September 23, 2026.

The exchange has halted new user registrations, while restrictions on opening new positions will begin on August 26. ⚠️

Parent company… pic.twitter.com/hVnj359KZY

— TheNewsCrypto (@The_NewsCrypto) July 23, 2026

During the final weeks, the platform will gradually force-close remaining positions, while any contracts still open when trading ends on 23 September will be closed automatically.

Contracts with lower liquidity may also be settled earlier under the exchange's existing settlement procedures, with advance notice provided where applicable.

Withdrawals Will Continue But Fees Apply After Closure

Customers will continue to have access to their accounts after trading stops, although only to view balances, transaction history and withdraw remaining assets.

BitMEX advised users not to delay withdrawals.

Verified customers who leave funds on the platform after 23 September 2026 will be charged the higher of a monthly fee equivalent to US$50 or 1% annually, with deductions made each month.

Source: BitMEX

The company also warned these custody charges could increase in the future after prior notice.

The exchange further cautioned users to remain alert for phishing attempts linked to the shutdown announcement.

It stressed that no priority withdrawal service exists and warned customers to ignore anyone claiming they could speed up access to funds.

BitMEX added that extra withdrawal reviews and heavy demand, together with blockchain confirmation times, particularly on Bitcoin, could result in slower processing during the transition period.

Exchange Leaves Behind a Lasting Trading Legacy

Founded in 2014 by Arthur Hayes, Benjamin Delo and Samuel Reed, BitMEX helped make perpetual swap contracts a standard product across the crypto industry.

Its 100x leveraged perpetual swaps were later adopted by many competing derivatives exchanges.

The company also highlighted its security history, stating that no customer funds had ever been lost to a hack throughout its more than 11 years of operation.

“BitMEX invented the 100x leverage perpetual swap, the most traded product in the crypto industry – now adopted by thousands of people and exchanges.”

BitMEX is shutting down on September 23, 2026.

Before Binance, OKX or Hyperliquid, BitMEX was the exchange that changed how crypto traders used leverage.

Founded in 2014 by Arthur Hayes, Ben Delo and Samuel Reed with mostly friends-and-family funding, it launched while the… https://t.co/taaoYEFUG3

— BlockFlow (@BlockFlow_News) July 23, 2026

Despite its early success, BitMEX faced increasing competition over the years.
